title: Teaching Inside the Thread
subtitle: An AI-Centered Pedagogy

copy.step1_prompt: What is your teaching goal today?
copy.step1_subtext: Pick the area that best matches what you want to work on. You can refine the focus on the next screen.
copy.step2_prompt: What specific action do you want to take?
copy.step2_subtext: Pick the focus that best matches your subproblem. You can edit later.
copy.step2_other_placeholder: Describe the specific focus you have in mind (a sentence is fine).
copy.step3_prompt: Tell the agent enough to understand your course.
copy.step3_subtext: All fields except course topic are optional, though more context helps the agent.
copy.step4_prompt: What materials or evidence would help the agent do this work?
copy.step4_subtext: Check anything you have on hand. "I'm starting from scratch" is a fine answer.
copy.step4_other_placeholder: Briefly describe any other materials or constraints.
copy.review_prompt: Review your selections, then copy the prompt and open the Companion.
copy.review_subtext: Edit any section by clicking its Edit link. The prompt below is what gets copied.
copy.copy_button_label: Copy prompt and open the Companion
copy.bridge_header: Your prompt is on its way.
copy.bridge_confirmation: Your prompt is on your clipboard. The AMCIS Teaching Companion should have opened in a new tab. Paste the prompt as your first message there.
copy.bridge_prompt_textarea_label: Your assembled prompt (manual copy if needed):
copy.bridge_m_open_note: This prompt is your M-Open, the start of a multi-turn exchange. When you're done, you can paste an M-Close into the thread or simply close it.
copy.reflection_subtext: Share a short reflection on what you tried and what shifted. The Q and O blocks below are optional but valued.
copy.reflection_textarea_placeholder: Write a sentence or two about what you tried and what changed.
copy.reflection_confirmation: Thank you. Your reflection is saved.
copy.try_x_label: Try an X block: open a free exploration
copy.x_prompt: If you have time, try an X block.
copy.x_subtext: Open a free exploration with the agent. Type ### X as your first line and ask anything the workshop didn't address. The thread captures it; we don't grade it.
copy.x_reopen_label: Re-open the Companion in a new tab
copy.no_materials_text: No specific materials yet, starting from scratch.

course_design.label: Course design and curriculum architecture
course_design.description: Syllabus work, learning outcomes, course-level AI policy, curriculum mapping, course restructuring, new course design.

teaching_is_concepts.label: Teaching IS concepts, methods, and technologies
teaching_is_concepts.description: Technical lecture redesign, AI-code-debugging exercises, AI-co-pilot labs, progressive scaffolds, process redesign activities, governance and accountability units.

cases_scenarios.label: Cases, scenarios, and applied examples
cases_scenarios.description: Discipline-specific examples, AI-decision cases, case adaptation, AI-failure mini-cases, escalating sequences, platform-governance activities.

engagement_protocols.label: Engagement, interaction, and student-AI protocols
engagement_protocols.description: Discussion design, mixed human-AI activities, student-AI exchange protocols, debates, AI-limitation activities, AI in peer-review.

assessment_integrity.label: Assessment and academic integrity in the AI era
assessment_integrity.description: Assignment redesign, process-based rubrics, integrity-without-detection strategy, show-your-work requirements, oral defense components, feedback systems.

research_methods.label: Research methods, theory, and evidence
research_methods.description: Phenomenon-to-construct translation, theory-building exercises, AI-supported literature synthesis, evaluating AI-generated evidence, human-vs-AI qualitative coding, prompt reproducibility.

doctoral_supervision.label: Doctoral training, supervision, and mentoring
doctoral_supervision.description: AI-augmented qualifying exams, dissertation coaching, AI as teaching partner for methods, responsible-AI-writing norms, dissertation-protocol design, AI-supported feedback templates.

boundary_statements.cases_scenarios: Best for faculty who want to build cases, generate examples, or develop applied scenarios for classroom use.
boundary_statements.research_methods: Best for teaching students how IS research is designed, evaluated, and interpreted.
boundary_statements.doctoral_supervision: Best for mentoring doctoral students and supporting long-arc scholarly development.

course_design.label: Redesign learning outcomes for AI presence
Map AI integration across an existing curriculum
Restructure a course around AI-augmented work
Plan a new course from scratch with AI in mind

teaching_is_concepts.label: Redesign a technical lecture for an AI-using classroom
Build an exercise where students debug AI-generated code
Design a hands-on lab where AI is a co-pilot, not the solution
Create progressive scaffolds for a technical concept
Build a classroom activity around process redesign with AI support
Build a teaching unit on data governance, privacy, or algorithmic accountability

cases_scenarios.label: Generate discipline-specific examples for a concept
Build a case-based discussion around AI-augmented decision-making
Adapt a published case for AI-era classroom use
Create a mini-case that surfaces an AI failure mode
Develop a sequence of escalating-difficulty examples
Build a classroom activity around platform governance or digital transformation

engagement_protocols.label: Generate discussion questions for an AI-era topic
Plan an in-class activity that requires both human and AI work
Design a student-AI exchange protocol for an assignment
Build a structured debate or comparison exercise around an AI topic
Create a low-stakes activity that exposes AI limitations
Design a peer-review activity in which AI is a third participant

assessment_integrity.label: Redesign an assignment to remain meaningful under AI access
Build a rubric that assesses reasoning and process, not just output
Develop an integrity strategy that does not rely on detection
Create a "show your work" requirement that's hard to fake
Design an oral defense or in-class demonstration component
Build a process-based feedback system for AI-assisted work

research_methods.label: Teach students how to translate real-world phenomena into research constructs
Teach students to evaluate the reliability and limitations of AI-generated evidence
Compare human and AI-supported coding in qualitative analysis
Build exercises around prompt reproducibility and validity in research

doctoral_supervision.label: Design an AI-augmented qualifying exam approach
Build a dissertation-coaching workflow using AI
Develop a methods training module using AI as a teaching partner
Develop supervision norms for responsible AI-assisted research writing
Design protocols for AI-assisted dissertation work
Create AI-supported feedback templates for student research drafts

fields.label: Teaching context and constraints (optional)
fields.placeholder: e.g., Required course, no TA support, institution has a restrictive AI policy, students have varied technical backgrounds.

audit_syllabus.label: Your current syllabus (current semester or recent past semester)
A recent set of assignments or assessments from the course
Your institution's AI-use policy or guidelines (if any)
An AI-generated syllabus audit or redesign suggestion, if you've experimented with one before

redesign_outcomes.label: Your current learning outcomes (or program-level outcomes if course outcomes don't exist yet)
Examples of outcomes from peer courses or institutions
Recent student work that suggests where current outcomes are or aren't being met
A sense of which learning outcomes feel outdated, weakened, or newly important in the AI era

add_ai_policy.label: Your institution's or department's existing AI-use policy (if any)
A peer course's AI-use policy you've seen and want to learn from
Your sense of what student behaviors you want to encourage and discourage
Examples of AI-use questions, misunderstandings, or edge cases students have raised

map_curriculum.label: The current curriculum or program structure (course list, sequence, prerequisites)
Your understanding of where AI already appears across the courses you teach, coordinate, or know well
Program-level learning outcomes or accreditation requirements
A target for where AI should appear, intensify, or shift across the sequence

restructure_course.label: Your current course structure (modules, units, weeks)
A sense of which course components feel pre-AI and need rethinking
Examples of student work that shows how AI is changing what's possible (or what's problematic)
A target for what the restructured course should look like (more project work, different assessment structures, integrated AI tools)

plan_new_course.label: The topic, audience, and credit-hour structure for the new course
Adjacent courses or prerequisite courses in your program
Peer courses you've seen and want to learn from (in IS or elsewhere)
Your initial thoughts on the course's scope and structure (even rough notes)

redesign_technical_lecture.label: Your current lecture slides, notes, or outline for this topic
The technical concept or skill being taught
Your sense of how AI is already changing how students engage with this material
Examples of student questions, misconceptions, or confusion patterns from past offerings

debug_ai_code.label: A topic or programming concept where you'd want students to practice debugging
Sample working code in the language and toolchain your course uses
A set of common bugs students typically introduce or struggle to find
An AI-generated code example containing subtle errors, misleading logic, or incomplete solutions
The skill level you're calibrating the exercise to (intro, intermediate, advanced)

ai_copilot_lab.label: The lab topic or assignment specification
The specific skills students should develop independently (the non-AI core)
The points in the lab where AI assistance supports learning versus where it bypasses essential skill development
Past lab work where students leaned too heavily on AI and missed the underlying skill

progressive_scaffolds.label: Your current introduction or first-pass explanation of it
Examples of where students typically lose the thread or hit a conceptual wall
The eventual application or assessment the scaffolding builds toward

process_redesign_activity.label: A workflow or organizational process students will analyze
The course context and learning outcomes this activity serves
Examples of workflow or process redesign activities students have done before
Your sense of where AI fits productively into the redesign workflow versus where it doesn't

governance_unit.label: The specific topic within governance, privacy, or accountability you want to teach
Existing readings, cases, or current events that anchor the unit
Relevant regulatory or institutional context (GDPR, organizational data policies, AI use guidelines)
Examples of AI behavior that illustrate the topic concretely (biased outputs, privacy leakage, accountability gaps)
The course or curricular context this unit fits into

discipline_examples.label: The concept the examples will illustrate
Your discipline or sub-discipline (e.g., MIS, BIT, analytics, IS strategy, cybersecurity)
The student background and level the examples should match
Existing examples you've used before that worked well, plus any that fell flat

ai_decision_case.label: The decision domain the case will explore (organizational, ethical, strategic, technical)
The role students will take in the case (analyst, manager, designer, auditor)
Past cases, scenarios, or organizational situations you've used that involved uncertainty or competing priorities
The kind of AI involvement the case should depict (advisory, autonomous, collaborative, ambiguous)

adapt_published_case.label: The published or teaching case you're working from (business-school, IS, or industry-focused)
The original learning objectives the case was designed to teach
Your sense of where the case feels dated under current AI conditions
The new learning objectives you want to add about AI-era decision-making

ai_failure_minicase.label: The failure mode you want to illustrate (bias, hallucination, miscalibration, accountability gap)
A realistic business scenario where this failure could occur
The student role and the kind of reasoning or discussion the case should provoke
Examples of similar failures from news, research, or industry reports

escalating_examples.label: The concept or skill the sequence will build toward
Your sense of students' starting point before they encounter this material
Existing examples in the course this sequence will sit alongside
A target endpoint: what mastery looks like for this concept or skill

platform_governance_activity.label: The specific aspect of platform governance or digital transformation the activity will explore
A real-world platform, organization, or transformation initiative students will analyze
The student role and analytical lens (governance, strategy, ethics, design, operations)
Existing readings, cases, or news articles that anchor the topic

discussion_questions.label: The topic, case, or reading the discussion will center on
Your learning outcomes for the discussion session
Past discussion questions you've used that worked well (or didn't land)
The class format and time available for discussion (class size, duration, in-person or online)

human_ai_activity.label: The course topic or skill the activity will address
The class size, duration, and format constraints
Examples of activities you've used before that involved collaboration, peer interaction, or role structure
Your sense of which parts of the activity must be human-driven versus where AI can productively participate

exchange_protocol.label: The assignment specification and learning outcomes
The specific AI tools students will be using
Past student-AI exchanges or transcripts you've encountered in teaching, discussion, or shared student work
Your sense of what productive student-AI interaction looks like for this kind of work
Examples of student-AI exchanges that went poorly or revealed problems

structured_debate.label: The topic or question the debate will center on
The positions, perspectives, or comparisons students will work with
Past debate or comparison exercises you've run (formats, structures, time allocations)
Source materials (readings, cases, AI outputs) students will use to develop their positions

expose_limitations.label: A topic, task, or domain area where AI limitations would be educationally useful to expose
An AI output (or several) demonstrating the limitation
Your sense of what students should learn from encountering the limitation
The course context this activity fits into

ai_peer_review.label: The assignment or work students will be reviewing
Your current peer-review protocol (if you have one) or a peer's
The specific role AI will play (reviewer, organizer, summarizer, comparator)
Past peer-review experiences that revealed where the process supported learning well, or where it became superficial or uneven
Examples of AI-generated review feedback, if you've experimented with it

redesign_assignment.label: The current assignment specification and rubric
Your sense of which parts of the assignment AI can now do trivially
Recent student submissions where AI appeared to be doing most of the intellectual work
An AI output run against the assignment, for comparison with student work
The course learning outcome this assignment is supposed to assess

process_rubric.label: Your current rubric for the assignment or assessment type
The course outcomes or skills you actually want to evaluate
Examples of student work where the final output looked fine but the reasoning was weak (or vice versa)
A peer rubric you've seen that handles process well

integrity_no_detection.label: A specific assignment or assessment you're worried about
Past incidents or near-incidents that shaped your current thinking (anonymized if needed)
Your sense of which student behaviors raise genuine integrity concerns versus which ones challenge traditional expectations

show_your_work.label: The assignment or assessment you want to add this requirement to
Examples of "show your work" attempts that didn't work (process narratives students wrote that felt formulaic or generated)
Examples of process evidence that did feel authentic or meaningful
Your sense of what process artifacts feel meaningful in your discipline (drafts, version histories, code commits, query logs, conversation logs, prototypes)

oral_defense.label: The current assignment or assessment this component would attach to
Your class size and time constraints (the operational reality)
Past in-class demonstration formats that worked or didn't (yours or peer examples)
The specific skill or understanding you want to verify in person

feedback_system.label: The assignment or coursework this feedback system would serve
Your current feedback workflow (what you give, when, how)
Examples of feedback approaches that helped students improve, and others that led to superficial revision or dependence
An AI-generated feedback attempt on similar work, for comparison
Your sense of where you want to be more or less directive in your feedback

phenomenon_to_construct.label: A phenomenon description or practitioner observation (news article, workplace anecdote, implementation problem, case vignette, or field observation)
Examples of published papers that operationalized a similar phenomenon as a construct
Past student attempts at construct definitions, including ones that revealed common misconceptions or conflated concepts
A target outcome: what a successful student response would look like (a paper, proposal, or analysis you'd accept)

theory_building_exercise.label: A phenomenon, paper, or case students will engage with
One or two theoretical exemplars from IS literature you'd want students to learn from or extend (e.g., TAM, sociomateriality, affordance theory)
Your course's stated learning outcome for theory engagement
Past student attempts at theory work that revealed common misconceptions or weak construct distinctions

lit_synthesis_workflows.label: The research question students are working on
A set of 5 to 15 papers students are expected to engage with
A draft annotated bibliography (yours or a student's)
An example of an AI-generated synthesis that went wrong (hallucinated citations, surface-level reading, missed connections)
The course or methods context this synthesis activity fits into

evaluate_ai_evidence.label: A topic where you've seen AI produce confidently wrong or biased output
A set of AI responses to a single prompt (showing variation)
A rubric or set of criteria for evaluating evidence in your field
A paper or methodological treatment students could compare AI output against

human_vs_ai_coding.label: A dataset of qualitative material (interview excerpts, open-ended survey responses, field notes, even a small set)
An existing coding scheme or codebook
A set of human-coded examples for comparison
An AI output that coded the same material (or a plan to generate one)
A learning outcome for the methods unit this exercise serves

prompt_reproducibility.label: A research question or analytic task you want students to use AI for
A set of prompts (yours or sourced) that produced different results on the same task
A criterion or rubric for what "good" output looks like
A small dataset or evidence base students can run the prompts against
What you want students to learn about reproducibility or prompt sensitivity

qual_exam_approach.label: Your program's current qualifying exam format and reading lists
Past qualifying exam questions (yours or your program's)
A sense of which students typically struggle and where
An AI response to a qualifying-exam-style question, for comparison and discussion

dissertation_coaching.label: An advisee's current dissertation draft or proposal (at any stage)
Your usual rhythm for advisee feedback (frequency, depth, written vs. meetings)
Dissertation drafts, timelines, or supervision approaches that worked well in the past
A clear sense of where this advisee is stuck or making slow progress

methods_training_module.label: The methods topic this module covers (qualitative, design science, econometrics, survey, experimental, computational)
Your existing teaching materials for this topic
The methods concept or skill students typically struggle with most
A current syllabus for the doctoral seminar this fits into
Examples of past student work that revealed where the teaching wasn't landing

writing_norms.label: Your department's or program's current AI-writing policy (if any)
Your own current supervision style and existing norms (even if unwritten)
Examples of student-written work with and without AI assistance
The journal venues your students typically target (each has its own AI disclosure norms now)
A draft authorship and disclosure agreement, if one exists in your program

dissertation_protocols.label: An advisee's dissertation topic and current chapter or methods section
The specific stages of dissertation work where AI use is being considered (literature, analysis, drafting, revision)
Your program's current expectations about disclosure of AI use
A draft or sample of AI-assisted work the advisee has already produced
A target venue or departmental expectation for what acceptable AI use should look like in the final dissertation

feedback_templates.label: A recent draft from an advisee or doctoral student
The kind of feedback you typically give (developmental, structural, methodological, line-level)
Examples of written feedback you've given on similar drafts
An AI-generated feedback attempt on a similar draft (showing what AI does well and where it misses)

default.label: Any existing course materials related to your goal
Recent assignments, assessments, or activities relevant to your goal
Your institution's AI-use policy or guidelines (if any)
Past student work or feedback that shaped your thinking

q_block.label: Which of these AI-in-teaching practices feels most likely to change something about your teaching this fall?

o_block.label: Rank these dimensions of AI-assisted teaching by how much they concern you.
